3494	
3495	\begin{lemma}[Colimit preservation gives epimorphism preservation]
3496	  \label{lem:preservesEpi_of_preservesColimitsOfShape_mathlib}
3497	  \lean{CategoryTheory.preservesEpimorphisms_of_preservesColimitsOfShape}
3498	  \mathlibok
3499	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3500	  Let \(F : \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{D}\) be a functor that preserves colimits of shape
3501	  \(\mathrm{WalkingSpan}\) (equivalently, preserves pushouts). Then \(F\) preserves epimorphisms: if
3502	  \(f\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathcal{C}\) then \(F(f)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathcal{D}\). In
3503	  particular a functor preserving finite colimits preserves epimorphisms, since
3504	  \(\mathrm{WalkingSpan}\) is a finite shape.
3505	\end{lemma}
3506	
3507	\begin{lemma}[The underlying-abelian-sheaf functor preserves finite colimits]
3508	  \label{lem:toSheaf_preservesFiniteColimits}
3509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.toSheaf_preservesFiniteColimits}
3510	  \uses{lem:sheafificationCompToSheaf_mathlib, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}
3511	  \textit{Project-bespoke gap-fill (the missing right-exact dual of \(\mathrm{toSheaf}\)).}
3512	  The forgetful functor \(\mathrm{toSheaf}\,R : \mathrm{Mod}(R) \to \mathrm{Sh}(J, \mathbf{Ab})\)
3513	  from sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to the underlying sheaves of abelian groups preserves
3514	  finite colimits. This is the right-exact counterpart of the limit-side fact that
3515	  \(\mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\) preserves finite limits; Mathlib supplies the latter but not the former, so
3516	  it is built here.
3517	\end{lemma}
3518	\begin{proof}
3519	  \uses{lem:sheafificationCompToSheaf_mathlib, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}
3520	  Write \(L := \operatorname{sheafification}(\mathrm{id}_{R_0}) : \mathrm{PreMod}(R_0) \to
3521	  \mathrm{Mod}(R)\) for the module-sheafification functor; \(L\) is a left adjoint whose counit is an
3522	  isomorphism, so it is a localization that preserves all colimits. Throughout, \(\circ\) denotes
3523	  diagrammatic composition: \(F \circ G\) means ``apply \(F\), then \(G\)''.
3524	
3525	  \emph{Step 1 (the composite \(L \circ \mathrm{toSheaf}\) preserves finite colimits).} By the
3526	  square isomorphism (Lemma~\ref{lem:sheafificationCompToSheaf_mathlib}),
3527	  \(L \circ \mathrm{toSheaf}\,R \cong \mathrm{toPresheaf}\,R_0 \circ
3528	  \operatorname{sheafify}_J^{\mathrm{Ab}}\). The forgetful functor \(\mathrm{toPresheaf}\,R_0\) on
3529	  presheaves of modules preserves finite colimits (colimits of presheaves of modules are computed
3530	  objectwise on the underlying abelian groups), and abelian-group sheafification
3531	  \(\operatorname{sheafify}_J^{\mathrm{Ab}}\) is a left adjoint, hence preserves all colimits.
3532	  Therefore their composite, and so the isomorphic functor \(L \circ \mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\), preserves
3533	  finite colimits.
3534	
3535	  \emph{Step 2 (descend from the composite to \(\mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\) alone).} Let
3536	  \(\iota : \mathrm{Mod}(R) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PreMod}(R_0)\) be the inclusion of sheaves of
3537	  modules into presheaves of modules, so that \(L \dashv \iota\) is the sheafification adjunction
3538	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}). The descent proceeds in three clauses.
3539	
3540	  \emph{(i) The counit is an isomorphism.} Every sheaf of modules is already a sheaf, so its
3541	  sheafification recovers it: the counit of \(L \dashv \iota\) is a natural isomorphism
3542	  \[
3543	    \varepsilon : \iota \circ L \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{Mod}(R)} .
3544	  \]
3545	
3546	  \emph{(ii) \(\mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\) is a retract of \(L \circ \mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\).} Whiskering the

3769	  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\) be an affine scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
3770	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Writing \(M = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global sections,
3771	  there is a natural isomorphism \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{M}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
3772	  under which \(\Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F}) = M_f\) for every \(f \in R\); in particular the sections of
3773	  \(\mathcal{F}\) over a distinguished open \(D(f)\) are the away localisation \(M_f\). This is the
3774	  affine quasi-coherent structure theorem (Tag 01HV, with the equivalence quasi-coherent-on-affine
3775	  \(\simeq\) \(R\)-modules, Tag 01I8).

3777	\begin{proof}
3778	  \uses{def:standard_affine_cover, lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections_of_presentation}
3779	  On an affine scheme the global-sections functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\) and
3780	  the tilde functor \(M \mapsto \widetilde{M}\) are mutually inverse equivalences between
3781	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and \(R\)-modules; the counit
3782	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})} \to \mathcal{F}\) is an isomorphism for quasi-coherent
3783	  \(\mathcal{F}\). By Tag 01HV the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over \(D(f)\) are \(M_f\), so the
3784	  isomorphism identifies \(\Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) with \(M_f\).
3785	
3786	  Concretely, the isomorphism is the inverse of the tilde–\(\Gamma\) counit: once the counit
3787	  \(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma} : \widetilde{\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})} \to \mathcal{F}\) is known to
3788	  be an isomorphism, the asserted iso \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})}\) is its
3789	  inverse (the one-line conditional argument formalized). The hypothesis that the counit is an
3790	  isomorphism holds for any \(\mathcal{F}\) admitting a global presentation
3791	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections_of_presentation}), and unconditionally for quasi-coherent
3792	  \(\mathcal{F}\) once the 01I8 instance \([{\,}\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\,\mathcal{F}{\,}] \Rightarrow
3793	  \operatorname{IsIso}(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma})\) is supplied (see the decomposition remark
3794	  below).
3795	\end{proof}

3868	  The unconditional quasi-coherent statement

3870	  \widetilde{\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})}\) reduces, through
3871	  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections}, to the single instance
3872	  \([{\,}\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\,\mathcal{F}{\,}] \Rightarrow
3873	  \operatorname{IsIso}(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma})\) on the affine base \(\operatorname{Spec} R\).
3874	  This is the content of Stacks Tag 01I8, organised here as a three-step route into the
3875	  presentation-form discharge:
3876	  \begin{enumerate}
3877	  \item \emph{Global generation.} A quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) is
3878	    generated by its global sections: one produces a global epimorphism
3879	    \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(I)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Quasi-coherence gives generators only
3880	    locally on a basic-open cover; these are globalised using the quasi-compactness of
3881	    \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) and the localisation-of-sections property of quasi-coherent sheaves
3882	    (sections over \(D(f)\) are the away localisation), the affine global-generation statement of
3883	    Tag 01I8. This is realised by the finite basic-open subcover
3884	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover}, P0), the localised-sections core
3885	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_localized_sections}, P1), and their assembly into a global generating
3886	    family (Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_global_generation}, P2).
3887	  \item \emph{Presentation.} The kernel of \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(I)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is
3888	    again quasi-coherent on \(\operatorname{Spec} R\), hence again globally generated by step (1);
3889	    composing yields a global presentation \(\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{Presentation}\). The kernel's
3890	    quasi-coherence uses the exactness of \(\widetilde{(-)}\)
3891	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:tilde_preserves_kernels}) to land at Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_kernel_qcoh} (P3); the
3892	    two generating families \(\sigma, \tau\) are assembled into the counit-iso input by
3893	    Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_of_genSections}.
3894	  \item \emph{Counit iso.} Feeding that presentation to
3895	    Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_of_presentation} makes
3896	    \(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma}\) an isomorphism; equivalently, this is the discharge of
3897	    Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections_of_presentation}. Packaged as the unconditional
3898	    instance, this is Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_of_quasicoherent} (P4), which upgrades
3899	    Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections} to its unconditional quasi-coherent form through

4200	\begin{proof}
4201	  \uses{lem:qcoh_localized_sections, lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover}
4202	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover} pick a finite basic-open cover
4203	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_{j=1}^{n} D(f_j)\) on each of which
4204	  \(\mathcal{F}|_{D(f_j)} \cong \widetilde{M_j}\). Each \(M_j\) is generated, as an
4205	  \(R_{f_j}\)-module, by local sections \(t \in \Gamma(D(f_j), \mathcal{F})\). By the
4206	  surjectivity half of Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_localized_sections}, after multiplying by a
4207	  suitable power \(f_j^{N}\) each such local section is the restriction of a global
4208	  section \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\); since \(f_j\) is a unit on \(D(f_j)\), these
4209	  global sections still generate \(\mathcal{F}\) over \(D(f_j)\). Collecting the finitely
4210	  many resulting global sections over all \(j\) gives a family that generates
4211	  \(\mathcal{F}\) on every member of the cover, hence everywhere: the induced map
4212	  \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(I)} \to \mathcal{F}\) is surjective on each \(D(f_j)\) and so is an
4213	  epimorphism of sheaves.
4214	\end{proof}

4216	\begin{lemma}[The tilde functor preserves kernels]
4217	  \label{lem:tilde_preserves_kernels}
4218	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tildePreservesFiniteLimits, AlgebraicGeometry.tilde_preservesFiniteColimits, AlgebraicGeometry.tilde_toStalk_map_injective, AlgebraicGeometry.tilde_preservesFiniteLimits_of_preservesKernels}
4219	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, \texttt{lemma-kernel-cokernel-quasi-coherent}
4220	  % (proof) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1425--1432).
4221	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "This follows from the exactness of the functor $\widetilde{\ }$
4222	  % since by Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions} we know that any map
4223	  % $\psi : \widetilde{M} \to \widetilde{N}$ comes from
4224	  % an $R$-module map $\varphi : M \to N$. (So we have
4225	  % $\Ker(\psi) = \widetilde{\Ker(\varphi)}$ and
4226	  % $\Coker(\psi) = \widetilde{\Coker(\varphi)}$.)"
4227	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes,
4228	  \texttt{lemma-kernel-cokernel-quasi-coherent} (and Tag 01HV, exactness of
4229	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\)).}
4230	  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\). The functor \(M \mapsto \widetilde{M}\) from
4231	  \(R\)-modules to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is exact; in particular it preserves
4232	  kernels: for an \(R\)-module map \(\varphi : M \to N\) the induced
4233	  \(\widetilde{\varphi} : \widetilde{M} \to \widetilde{N}\) has
4234	  \(\operatorname{Ker}(\widetilde{\varphi}) = \widetilde{\operatorname{Ker}(\varphi)}\),
4235	  and more generally \(\widetilde{(-)}\) carries finite limits of \(R\)-modules to the
4236	  corresponding limits of associated sheaves. The tilde functor is already known to be
4237	  additive and to preserve colimits; its preservation of finite limits (kernels) is the
4238	  property needed here and is supplied as a dedicated lemma.
4239	\end{lemma}

4241	% NOTE: The Lean instance AlgebraicGeometry.tildePreservesFiniteLimits
4242	% (PreservesFiniteLimits / PreservesKernels for the tilde functor
4243	% ~ : ModuleCat R ⥤ (Spec R).Modules) is ABSENT from Mathlib and is
4244	% project-to-build (Route-P P3 sub-gap). Mathlib provides the tilde functor
4245	% and its stalk computation but not the packaged finite-limit-preservation
4246	% instance; the informal proof below is the stalkwise-flatness argument the
4247	% eventual prover formalizes.
4248	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Let us compute the stalk of $\widetilde M$ at a point
4249	% $x \in \Spec(R)$. Suppose that $x$ corresponds to the prime
4250	% $\mathfrak p \subset R$. By definition of the stalk we see that
4251	% $\widetilde M_x = \colim_{f\in R, f\not\in \mathfrak p} M_f$ ...
4252	% Hence by Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-localization-colimit}
4253	% we conclude that $\widetilde M_x = M_{\mathfrak p}$."
4254	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The exactness of the functor $M \mapsto \widetilde M$
4255	% follows from the fact that the functor $M \mapsto M_{\mathfrak p}$
4256	% is exact and the fact that exactness of short exact sequences
4257	% may be checked on stalks, see Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-abelian}."
4258	\begin{proof}
4259	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (item~(7) and
4260	  proof), Tag 01HV.}
4261	  Kernels in the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules are computed
4262	  objectwise and sheafified, and isomorphisms of sheaves may be checked on stalks: a
4263	  morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is an isomorphism if and only if it induces an
4264	  isomorphism on every stalk. So it suffices to compare stalks. By the stalk computation
4265	  for the tilde functor (\texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} item~(7)) the stalk of
4266	  \(\widetilde{M}\) at a point \(x \in \operatorname{Spec} R\) corresponding to the prime
4267	  \(\mathfrak{p}\) is the localisation
4268	  \(\widetilde{M}_x = M_{\mathfrak{p}}\), naturally in \(M\): the stalk functor at \(x\)
4269	  applied to \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is the localisation functor \(M \mapsto M_{\mathfrak{p}}\).
4270	
4271	  Now let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be an \(R\)-module map with kernel
4272	  \(K = \operatorname{Ker}(\varphi)\), giving the exact sequence
4273	  \(0 \to K \to M \xrightarrow{\varphi} N\). Localisation \(R \to R_{\mathfrak{p}}\) is
4274	  flat, hence the functor \(M \mapsto M_{\mathfrak{p}}\) is exact and in particular
4275	  preserves kernels: \(K_{\mathfrak{p}} = \operatorname{Ker}(\varphi_{\mathfrak{p}} :
4276	  M_{\mathfrak{p}} \to N_{\mathfrak{p}})\). Translating through the stalk identification,
4277	  the natural comparison map
4278	  \(\widetilde{K} \to \operatorname{Ker}(\widetilde{\varphi})\) induces, at every point
4279	  \(x \leftrightarrow \mathfrak{p}\), the isomorphism \(K_{\mathfrak{p}} \xrightarrow{\sim}
4280	  \operatorname{Ker}(\varphi_{\mathfrak{p}})\) (using that the stalk functor, being a
4281	  filtered colimit, is itself exact and commutes with forming the kernel). Being a
4282	  stalkwise isomorphism, the comparison map is an isomorphism of sheaves, so
4283	  \(\operatorname{Ker}(\widetilde{\varphi}) = \widetilde{\operatorname{Ker}(\varphi)}\).
4284	  The same stalkwise-flatness argument applied to a finite diagram of \(R\)-modules shows
4285	  that \(\widetilde{(-)}\) carries finite limits to finite limits; equivalently
4286	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left exact, i.e.\ preserves finite limits (kernels).
4287	\end{proof}

4289	\begin{lemma}[The kernel of a generating epimorphism is quasi-coherent]
4290	  \label{lem:qcoh_kernel_qcoh}
4291	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcoh_kernel_qcoh}
4292	  \uses{lem:qcoh_global_generation, lem:tilde_preserves_kernels, lem:free_isQuasicoherent}
4293	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, \texttt{lemma-kernel-cokernel-quasi-coherent}
4294	  % (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1418--1432).
4295	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
4296	  % Kernels and cokernels of maps of quasi-coherent
4297	  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules are quasi-coherent."
4298	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes,
4299	  \texttt{lemma-kernel-cokernel-quasi-coherent}.}
4300	  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\), let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent
4301	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let \(\sigma : \mathcal{F}.\mathrm{GeneratingSections}\)
4302	  be the generating family of Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_global_generation}, with epimorphism
4303	  \(\sigma.\pi : \mathcal{O}_X^{(I)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Then
4304	  \(\operatorname{Ker}(\sigma.\pi)\) is again quasi-coherent on \(\operatorname{Spec} R\),
4305	  and therefore — by Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_global_generation} applied to it — globally
4306	  generated, yielding a second generating family
4307	  \(\tau : (\operatorname{Ker} \sigma.\pi).\mathrm{GeneratingSections}\).
4308	\end{lemma}
4309	\begin{proof}
4310	  \uses{lem:qcoh_global_generation, lem:tilde_preserves_kernels, lem:free_isQuasicoherent}
4311	  The free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(I)}\) is quasi-coherent
4312	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:free_isQuasicoherent}) and \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent by
4313	  hypothesis. On each member \(D(f_j)\) of a finite trivialising cover both source and
4314	  target are of the form \(\widetilde{(-)}\), and the map \(\sigma.\pi\) corresponds to an
4315	  \(R_{f_j}\)-module map; since \(\widetilde{(-)}\) preserves kernels
4316	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:tilde_preserves_kernels}),
4317	  \(\operatorname{Ker}(\sigma.\pi)|_{D(f_j)}\) is the associated sheaf of the kernel of
4318	  that module map, hence quasi-coherent locally on the cover. Quasi-coherence is local,

4053	\begin{lemma}[\(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) is local on a finite spanning cover]
4054	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover}
4055	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover,
4056	    AlgebraicGeometry.exists_sum_pow_eq_one, AlgebraicGeometry.mem_range_of_span_pow,
4057	    AlgebraicGeometry.eq_zero_of_span_pow, AlgebraicGeometry.map_smul_endFun,
4058	    AlgebraicGeometry.bump_eq, AlgebraicGeometry.per_j_surj, AlgebraicGeometry.per_j_eq}
4059	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(M\) and \(N\) be \(R\)-modules, let
4060	  \(g : M \to N\) be an \(R\)-linear map, let \(f \in R\), and let
4061	  \(s : \{1, \ldots, n\} \to R\) be a finite family with
4062	  \(\operatorname{span}\{s_1, \ldots, s_n\} = R\) (the unit ideal). For each \(j\) write
4063	  \(M_{s_j} = (\operatorname{powers} s_j)^{-1} M\) and \(N_{s_j} = (\operatorname{powers}
4064	  s_j)^{-1} N\) for the localisations at the powers of \(s_j\), and let
4065	  \(g_{s_j} : M_{s_j} \to N_{s_j}\) be the induced \(R\)-linear (equivalently
4066	  \(R_{s_j}\)-linear) map. Suppose that for every \(j\) the localised map \(g_{s_j}\)
4067	  exhibits \(N_{s_j}\) as the localisation of \(M_{s_j}\) at the powers of (the image of)
4068	  \(f\), i.e.\ \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g_{s_j}\). Then
4069	  \(g\) itself exhibits \(N\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the powers of \(f\):
4070	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g\).
4071	\end{lemma}
4072	\begin{proof}
4073	
4074	  This is a purely commutative-algebra statement: the predicate
4075	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g\) is the conjunction of its
4076	  three defining conditions, and each is verified by descent along the spanning cover. Throughout we use the standard partition
4077	  of unity: since \(\operatorname{span}\{s_j\} = R\), for any chosen exponents \(N_j\) the
4078	  powers \(s_j^{N_j}\) again span the unit ideal, so there are \(r_j \in R\) with
4079	  \(\sum_j r_j s_j^{N_j} = 1\); and an element of an \(R\)-module is zero iff its image in
4080	  \(M_{s_j}\) vanishes for every \(j\) (locality of being zero on a spanning cover, the
4081	  \(s_j\)-torsion form of the same partition of unity).
4082	
4083	  \emph{\(f\) acts invertibly on \(N\).} For each \(j\), the hypothesis
4084	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g_{s_j}\) makes multiplication
4085	  by \(f\) on \(N_{s_j}\) bijective. Multiplication by \(f\) on \(N\) is an isomorphism iff it
4086	  is so after localising at each \(s_j\) (an \(R\)-linear endomorphism is bijective iff it is
4087	  bijective on the cover \(\{D(s_j)\}\), since injectivity and surjectivity are both local on
4088	  a spanning cover); hence \(f \cdot (-) : N \to N\) is bijective, and likewise every power
4089	  \(f^k\).
4090	
4091	  \emph{Every \(y \in N\) satisfies \(f^k y = g(x)\) for some \(x \in M\), \(k\).}
4092	  Fix \(y \in N\). For each \(j\), surjectivity of \(g_{s_j}\) as a localisation at the powers
4093	  of \(f\) yields \(m_j \in M\), exponents \(a_j\) (clearing the \(s_j\)-denominator) and
4094	  \(k_j\) with \(s_j^{a_j} f^{k_j} y = g(m_j)\) holding after localising at \(s_j\); clearing
4095	  the remaining \(s_j\)-torsion (enlarging \(a_j\)) we may take this as an honest equality
4096	  \(s_j^{a_j} f^{k_j} y = g(m_j)\) in \(N\). Choose a common \(k = \max_j k_j\) (replacing
4097	  \(m_j\) by \(f^{k - k_j} m_j\)) so that \(s_j^{a_j} f^{k} y = g(m_j)\) for all \(j\). Pick,
4098	  by the partition of unity, \(r_j\) with \(\sum_j r_j s_j^{a_j} = 1\) (possible because the
4099	  \(s_j^{a_j}\) span the unit ideal). Then
4100	  \(f^{k} y = \sum_j r_j s_j^{a_j} f^{k} y = \sum_j r_j\, g(m_j) = g\bigl(\sum_j r_j m_j\bigr)\),
4101	  so \(x = \sum_j r_j m_j\) and the exponent \(k\) work.
4102	
4103	  \emph{If \(g(x) = g(x')\) then \(f^k(x - x') = 0\) for some \(k\).} Put
4104	  \(z = x - x'\), so \(g(z) = 0\). For each \(j\), the equaliser clause of
4105	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g_{s_j}\) gives, from the
4106	  vanishing of \(g_{s_j}(z) = 0\) in \(N_{s_j}\), an exponent \(k_j\) with
4107	  \(f^{k_j} z = 0\) after localising at \(s_j\); clearing the \(s_j\)-denominator there is
4108	  \(b_j\) with \(s_j^{b_j} f^{k_j} z = 0\) in \(M\). Take \(k = \max_j k_j\), so
4109	  \(s_j^{b_j} f^{k} z = 0\) for all \(j\). With \(r_j\) chosen so that
4110	  \(\sum_j r_j s_j^{b_j} = 1\),
4111	  \(f^{k} z = \sum_j r_j s_j^{b_j} f^{k} z = 0\). Hence \(f^{k}(x - x') = 0\).
4112	
4113	  The three clauses are exactly the data of
4114	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g\).
4115	\end{proof}
